section.title: Friendship with the World
section.sectionRationale: The wisdom from below now shows its fruit: quarrels and fights among believers. James traces conflict to warring desires, unmasks worldliness as spiritual adultery and enmity with God, and then, at the letter's turning point, sounds the gospel of grace to the humble. He issues a string of urgent calls to repent, submit, and draw near, and warns against the pride of slander and judging that usurps God's throne.
section.thesis: Fights among God's people flow from selfish cravings and friendship with the world, which is enmity against God; yet He gives more grace to the humble, so we must submit, draw near, and repent, ceasing to judge as though we were the Lawgiver.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the source of quarrels in warring desire; friendship with the world and the greater grace God gives; and the call to humble repentance and to stop judging one another.

verses.text: What causes conflicts and quarrels among you? Don’t they come from the {{hedone|passions}} at war within you?
You crave what you do not have; you kill and covet, but are unable to obtain it. You quarrel and fight. You do not have, because you do not ask.
And when you do ask, you do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, that you may squander it on your pleasures.

units.label: Friendship with the world
units.note: enmity with God, yet more grace

verses.text: You adulteresses! Do you not know that {{philia|friendship}} with the world is hostility toward God? Therefore, whoever chooses to be a friend of the world renders himself an enemy of God.
Or do you think the Scripture says without reason that the Spirit He caused to dwell in us yearns with envy?
But He gives us more {{charis|grace}}. This is why it says: “God opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble.”
Submit yourselves, then, to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.
Draw near to God, and He will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded.
Grieve, mourn, and weep. Turn your laughter to mourning, and your joy to gloom.
{{tapeinos|Humble}} yourselves before the Lord, and He will exalt you.

Brothers, do not slander one another. Anyone who speaks against his brother or judges him speaks against the law and judges it. And if you judge the law, you are not a practitioner of the law, but a judge of it.
There is only one Lawgiver and Judge, the One who is able to save and destroy. But who are you to judge your neighbor?

hedone.gloss: Pleasure, desire, the craving for gratification; the root of the word hedonism. James says fights among believers come from these passions warring within, and that even prayers fail when aimed at squandering God's gifts on such pleasures. Disordered desire is the hidden engine of conflict.

philia.gloss: Friendship, affectionate love, close alliance. Friendship with the world means adopting its values and loves in opposition to God, and James calls it spiritual adultery and enmity with God. One cannot be the intimate friend of the world and the friend of God at once.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unmerited favor and enabling help. At the letter's turning point James declares that God gives more grace, greater than the pull of sin, to those who humble themselves. Grace is God's answer to the proud, self-seeking heart: He resists the proud but pours favor on the lowly.

tapeinos.gloss: Humble, lowly, of low position. God gives grace to the tapeinos and exalts those who humble themselves before Him. Humility is the posture that receives grace, the opposite of the pride God opposes; it is the doorway back from friendship with the world to fellowship with God.

basic.context: James asks where all the fighting in the church comes from, and he answers bluntly: from your own cravings at war inside you. You want things you cannot get, so you quarrel; you do not have because you do not ask, and when you do ask, you ask selfishly. Then he lands the hardest word: to be a friend of the world, cozy with its values against God, is spiritual adultery and makes you God's enemy. But right there, at the low point, comes the best news: God gives more grace. He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. So James pleads: submit to God, resist the devil, draw near, wash your hands, humble yourself, and stop setting yourself up as judge over others.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the turning point of the letter, and it is heavily prescriptive, packed with about ten imperatives calling for repentance. The key is to read it as a summons to humility that ends in grace, not merely a scolding. The shocking word adulteresses is covenant language: God's people are His bride, so loving the world is unfaithfulness. Verse 5 is famously hard to translate, but the flow is clear, God is a jealous lover of His people, and He gives greater grace.
basic.originalLanguages: The conflicts spring from hedone, pleasures or cravings, warring within. The root sin is philia, friendship, with the world, which is enmity toward God. Against the proud God sets charis, grace, more grace, given to the tapeinos, the humble. The passage moves from disordered desire to worldliness to the grace that meets the lowly.
basic.typology: The image of God's people as an unfaithful wife recalls the prophets, especially Hosea, and points to Christ the faithful Bridegroom who wins back an adulterous bride. Drawing near to God fulfills the longing of the old covenant worshiper and is opened fully through Christ. The exaltation of the humble anticipates the pattern of Christ, who humbled Himself and was exalted.
basic.christ: Grace to the humble is grace that comes through Christ, who Himself took the lowest place and was exalted, and who calls the weary to Himself. The faithful Bridegroom who reclaims an adulterous people is the Lord Jesus, who loved the church and gave Himself for her. Through Him alone can enemies of God draw near and be received.
basic.god: God is revealed as a jealous lover of His people, grieved by their friendship with the world, yet lavish in grace to the humble. He resists the proud and lifts up the lowly. He is the one Lawgiver and Judge, able to save and to destroy, so that human judging of others is a proud theft of His throne. Draw near to Him, James promises, and He will draw near to you.
basic.crossReferences: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble is quoted from Proverbs 3:34 and repeated in 1 Peter 5:5-6. Jesus said no one can serve two masters, God and the world, Matthew 6:24. James 4:10 echoes 1 Peter 5:6. And do not judge, lest you be judged, comes from Matthew 7:1.
basic.oneStory: God has always sought a faithful people and grieved over their flirtation with idols and the world, from Israel's spiritual adultery to the church's worldliness. James stands in that prophetic line but points to the gospel remedy: God gives more grace to the humble. The way home from being God's enemy is repentance and lowliness, opened by Christ the faithful Bridegroom, who humbled Himself, was exalted, and draws near to all who draw near to God.

context.literary: Chapter 4 flows directly from the two wisdoms: the earthly wisdom of envy and ambition now erupts in quarrels. Verses 1 to 3 diagnose the source, verses 4 to 6 confront worldliness and announce greater grace, and verses 7 to 10 issue a cascade of imperatives calling for repentance, with verses 11 to 12 applying humility to the sin of judging. This is widely seen as the letter's central appeal.
context.historical: James writes to scattered congregations marked by rivalry, favoritism, and dispute, some perhaps drawn to the ambition and acquisitiveness of the surrounding culture. The language of adultery and friendship with the world would strike Jewish-Christian readers steeped in the prophets' charge that Israel had played the harlot with idols.
context.canonical: The passage is saturated with Scripture: the prophetic image of spiritual adultery, the quotation of Proverbs 3:34, and clear echoes of Jesus' teaching on serving one master and not judging. Its promise that God gives grace to the humble becomes a touchstone repeated by Peter, forming a shared apostolic call to humility.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To trace the church's quarrels to selfish desire and worldliness, to confront friendship with the world as enmity with God, to proclaim that God gives greater grace to the humble, and to call for wholehearted repentance and an end to proud judging of others.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: After diagnosing the problem, the passage becomes intensely prescriptive: submit, resist, draw near, cleanse, purify, mourn, humble yourselves, do not slander or judge. These commands describe the path of repentance for a worldly heart, all resting on the promise that God gives grace to the humble.

debates.question: What does the difficult verse 5 mean about the Spirit yearning with envy?

views.label: God jealously yearns over the spirit He put in us
views.case: Many read it of God's own jealous love: the Spirit He caused to dwell in us yearns jealously for our undivided devotion, which fits the charge of adultery and the promise of greater grace. God is a jealous lover who will not share His bride with the world.

views.label: The human spirit tends toward envy
views.case: Others take it of the human spirit's bent to envious craving, describing the sinful pull that friendship with the world feeds. Either reading leads into verse 6: but He gives more grace.

debates.bearing: The verse is genuinely hard, but the argument is clear either way. Whether it speaks of God's jealousy for us or our bent to envy, the answer is the greater grace God gives to the humble.

debates.question: Does forbidding judging in verses 11 to 12 rule out all discernment?

views.label: No; it forbids proud, condemning judgment
views.case: James condemns slander and setting oneself up as judge over a brother, usurping the place of the one Lawgiver and Judge. It does not forbid the discernment and loving correction the letter itself calls for, but the self-exalting judgment that plays God.

views.label: It targets judging that dismisses God's law
views.case: Others stress the specific logic: to condemn a brother is to act as judge over the law itself, as if one stood above it. The sin is arrogating an authority that belongs to God alone.

debates.bearing: Both agree the ban is on proud, God-usurping condemnation, not on wise discernment or the restoration of a wanderer that James commends in chapter 5.

study.originalLanguages: James diagnoses the church's strife with a single word, hedone, pleasures or cravings, warring within and spilling out as quarrels and even prayers twisted to feed self-indulgence. The deeper disease is philia, friendship, with the world, an intimate alliance with its values that amounts to spiritual adultery and enmity with God. Then the gospel turns: God gives more charis, grace, greater than the power of sin, according to the quoted proverb that He resists the proud but gives grace to the tapeinos, the humble. The whole cascade of imperatives, submit, draw near, cleanse, humble yourselves, describes the lowliness that alone receives that grace.

typology.type: God's people as an unfaithful wife
typology.fulfillment: The prophetic image, as in Hosea, fulfilled in Christ the faithful Bridegroom who redeems and cleanses His bride, Ephesians 5:25-27.
The access longed for under the old covenant, opened fully through Christ, by whom we draw near, Hebrews 10:22.
The pattern of Christ, who humbled Himself and was exalted, Philippians 2:8-9, and of all who share His lowliness.

study.christ: The greater grace James proclaims comes to us in Christ, who humbled Himself to the point of death and was highly exalted, and who invites the lowly and weary to come to Him. He is the faithful Bridegroom who loves and cleanses an unfaithful people, giving Himself for the church. Because of Him, enemies of God can submit, draw near, and be received, and the promise stands that those who humble themselves before the Lord He will exalt, just as the Father exalted the Son.
study.god: God is a jealous lover of His people, wounded by their friendship with the world yet rich in grace toward the humble. He gives more grace, actively opposing the proud and lifting up the lowly. He is the one Lawgiver and Judge, uniquely able to save and to destroy, which is why our judging of one another is a proud usurpation of His unique authority. Yet He is near, promising to draw near to all who draw near to Him in repentance.

crossReferences.note: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, quoted in verse 6.
The same proverb applied with the call to humble yourselves under God's mighty hand, closely paralleling James.
No one can serve two masters, God and the world, behind the charge of friendship with the world.
God's people as an adulterous wife, the prophetic background to adulteresses in verse 4.
Do not judge, or you too will be judged, behind the warning of verses 11 to 12.

study.oneStory: God has always been a jealous husband to His people, grieved when they chase the world and its idols, from Israel's harlotry in the prophets to the church's worldliness here. James speaks in that prophetic voice but drives to the gospel: God gives more grace to the humble. The road back from enmity with God is repentance and lowliness, thrown wide open by Christ the faithful Bridegroom, who humbled Himself, was exalted, and draws near to everyone who draws near to God.

quiz.q: Where does James say the quarrels among believers come from?

options.text: From their own passions and cravings at war within them.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2: conflict springs from disordered desire inside us.

options.text: From wanting things they cannot get, so they fight to obtain them.
options.note: True to verse 2, describing how the craving works out.

options.text: From other people who provoke them unfairly.
options.note: James locates the root within, not merely in others.

options.text: From God withholding good things arbitrarily.
options.note: They lack because they do not ask, or ask wrongly, not because God is stingy.

quiz.q: Why does James call friendship with the world so serious?

options.text: Because it is spiritual adultery and makes one an enemy of God.
options.note: Verse 4: loving the world is enmity toward God.

options.text: Because one cannot be a friend of the world and of God at once.
options.note: True to the logic, echoing serving two masters.

options.text: Because the world has nothing good in it whatsoever.
options.note: The issue is divided love and allegiance, not that creation is evil.

options.text: Because God does not really mind where our loyalties lie.
options.note: The opposite: God is a jealous lover of His people.

quiz.q: What is the turning-point good news in verse 6?

options.text: God gives more grace; He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
options.note: Verse 6: greater grace for the lowly is the hinge of the passage.

options.text: God's grace is greater than the pull of our sin.
options.note: True to the promise, though it omits the proud-humble contrast.

options.text: God gives grace only to those who first prove themselves.
options.note: Grace goes to the humble, not the self-proven.

options.text: God leaves the proud and humble to the same fate.
options.note: He opposes the proud and gives grace to the humble, a real difference.

quiz.q: What does James command in verses 7 to 10?

options.text: Submit to God, resist the devil, draw near, and humble yourselves in repentance.
options.note: Verses 7 to 10: a cascade of calls to humble repentance.

options.text: Draw near to God with the promise that He will draw near to you.
options.note: True from verse 8, one part of the fuller call.

options.text: Wait passively for God to change you without any response.
options.note: The passage is full of active imperatives to repent and turn.

options.text: Assert yourself so that God will be forced to bless you.
options.note: The call is to humble yourself, and He will exalt you.

quiz.q: Why does James forbid judging one another in verses 11 to 12?

options.text: Because there is one Lawgiver and Judge, and to condemn a brother usurps God's place.
options.note: Verse 12: judging others steals the authority of the one Judge.

options.text: Because speaking against a brother sets oneself above the law itself.
options.note: True to verse 11, another angle on the same pride.

options.text: Because Christians should never discern right from wrong at all.
options.note: James forbids proud condemnation, not wise discernment.

options.text: Because no one ever does anything worth correcting.
options.note: The issue is who has the right to judge, not that sin is unreal.

questions: James traces fights to cravings at war within us. Behind a recent conflict of yours, what desire were you really fighting to protect or obtain?
Friendship with the world is subtle. Where have you grown comfortable with values or loves that quietly compete with your devotion to God?
God gives more grace to the humble. What would it look like this week to come to Him low and empty rather than proud and self-sufficient?
The passage calls us to draw near to God with the promise that He will draw near to us. What is one concrete way you could draw near this week?
Where are you tempted to sit as judge over a brother or sister? How does remembering the one Lawgiver and Judge change how you speak about them?

translationNotes.note: Passions renders hedone, pleasures or cravings; a footnote gives the literal passions warring among your members, picturing an internal war that erupts in outward conflict.
Adulteresses draws on the prophetic image of God's people as an unfaithful wife; a footnote points to Hosea 3:1. It frames worldliness as covenant unfaithfulness.

This verse is notoriously difficult; it may speak of God's jealous yearning over the spirit He placed in us, or of the human spirit's bent to envy. The following verse resolves it: but He gives more grace.
The quotation, God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, is from Proverbs 3:34, printed as poetry in the BSB and set here as running prose.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 4:1-12. The poetic couplet in verse 6 is set as running prose. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
